---
title: "Why AI agent demos break in production"
description: "The demo works and production doesn't, because the failures live in everything wrapped around the model, not in the model."
date: 2026-04-14
order: 1
---

{{< include ../_series.qmd >}}

Most AI agent demos fail in boring ways.

The agent forgets something the user said five turns ago. It treats a guess as a fact. It calls the wrong tool because the tool name sounded close enough. It returns a polished answer only loosely connected to the evidence. It retries a calendar write after a timeout and books the same meeting twice. Then, when someone asks what happened, the logs say:

```
tool_call: retrieval, status: success
```

Before something breaks, reliability sounds like a nice engineering virtue. After something breaks, the questions get sharper. *Why did the agent think that was true? Why did it call that tool? Why didn't it ask a clarifying question? Why did it retry the write? Why can't we reconstruct the run?*

The instinct at that point is to look at the model. Maybe a stronger one would help. Sometimes it does. More often it produces the same failures in slightly different shapes, because the failures were never really about the model.

The diagnostic move that organizes everything else in this series is to stop asking "how do we make the model better?" and start asking: *What exactly failed, and where should control have lived?*

That question is the beginning of harness engineering. This is the first article in a short series about building agent systems that survive real users, real tools, real ambiguity, and real debugging.

## Three small agents

It's easier to talk about reliability using concrete examples. The series uses three running examples throughout, each chosen because it makes a different class of failure especially visible.

The **customer support intake agent** holds a conversation with a user, gathers what's going on, identifies the kind of issue, and produces a structured handoff for a human. It does not solve the issue itself. It is conversational discovery without write authority.

The **scheduling assistant** books, reschedules, and cancels meetings on someone's calendar. It uses tools that change the world.

The **internal docs Q&A agent** answers questions over a corpus of company documentation. It retrieves passages and synthesizes a short response.

Six failure shapes show up across these three.

## Failure 1: The agent bluffs when it should pause

A user opens the intake conversation: "We're having issues with the new dashboard, and I think it's related to the migration we did last month, but I'm not totally sure."

A weak agent immediately starts triaging the migration. It asks about migration steps. It surfaces likely root causes. It drafts a handoff that pins the issue to the migration.

The user never said the migration was the cause. They said they thought it might be. The agent treated a hedge as a fact and built on it.

A reliable agent should notice the hedge and seek clarification: "Has the migration been confirmed as the cause, or is that still a guess?"

This is not about making the model smarter. The problem is that the system has no explicit behavior for uncertain facts.

To resolve this, the system needs a place to **record uncertainty**:

```python
state["root_cause"] = {
    "value": "migration",
    "confidence": "uncertain",
    "source": "user_hedged_statement",
}
```

And it also needs **behavior attached to that confidence label**: when an upcoming step depends on a fact still labeled `uncertain`, route to clarification instead of action.

The **visible failure** is "the agent jumped to conclusions." The **deeper failure** is that the system had no mechanism for treating uncertainty differently from certainty.

## Failure 2: The agent forgets what already happened

Same intake conversation. On turn 2, the user says: "We're on the enterprise plan, and the dashboard is the only feature we use." On turn 9, the agent asks: "Just to confirm, are you on the standard or enterprise plan?" On turn 12, the handoff record lists the plan as `unknown`.

That looks like a model deficiency. Most of the time it's a state architecture problem. The agent stuffed the whole transcript back into the context every turn and hoped the model would keep the right facts live in its head. That degrades fast for long messy conversations full of side topics.

The fix is to store the fact once, in structured state, the moment it's confirmed:

```python
state["customer"] = {
    "plan_tier": {
        "value": "enterprise",
        "confidence": "confirmed",
        "source_turn": 2,
    },
    "primary_feature": {
        "value": "dashboard",
        "confidence": "confirmed",
        "source_turn": 2,
    },
}
```

Now the agent doesn't have to rediscover reality from scratch on every turn. It can read the structured fact directly.

The **visible failure** is "the model forgot." The **deeper failure** is that the system treated the conversation as memory. But remember: the transcript is what the user sees, state is what the system relies on. Article 3 in this series goes deeper into the distinction.

## Failure 3: The agent takes the wrong action

The scheduling assistant has these tools:

```
list_calendar_events()
find_event_by_description()
reschedule_event()
cancel_event()
```

The user says: "Move my Tuesday review with Priya to Thursday."

A weak agent reaches for `cancel_event` followed by a fresh booking. Or it calls `reschedule_event` with a fuzzy description and the API matches the wrong event.

The right sequence is `find_event_by_description` first, a confirmation if more than one event matches, then `reschedule_event` against a specific event ID. **That sequence isn't something the model should have to invent fresh on every turn.**

The reliability question is not "can the model call tools?" It is: *can the system make the wrong action hard to take?*

The **visible failure** is "the agent picked the wrong tool." The **deeper failure** is that the system relied on the model to invent a safe tool sequence every time, instead of encoding the sequence in the harness.

## Failure 4: The agent sounds grounded, but it is not

The docs Q&A agent receives this question: "What is our policy on customer data retention for trial accounts?"

It retrieves five passages and returns a polished, confident answer saying 30 days. The corpus actually says 90. The right passage was in the retrieved set, but synthesis leaned on the wrong ones.

A reliable retrieval agent should make three things easy to verify: what evidence was used, what claims are supported by that evidence, and where the supporting evidence actually addresses the question being asked.

The way to get there is to keep evidence and claims separate, with an explicit mapping between them:

```python
supported_claims = [
    {
        "claim": "Trial account data is retained for 90 days after trial expiry.",
        "evidence_refs": ["policy_42:trial_accounts"],
        "confidence": "confirmed",
    }
]
```

Now synthesis writes from the supported claims, not from the raw passage pile.

The **visible failure** is "the agent gave a wrong answer." The **deeper failure** is that the system never tracked which claims were actually supported by which evidence. Groundedness is a property of the system, not a property of the prose. Article 5 walks through this in more detail.

## Failure 5: The agent repeats a side effect

The scheduling assistant calls `reschedule_event(event_id="evt_482", new_time="2026-05-12T14:00")`. The API takes thirty seconds and returns a timeout. The agent can't tell whether the reschedule went through. It retries. Now the meeting has been rescheduled twice and Priya has received two confusingly similar calendar updates.

Reads are cheap to retry. Writes are not.

Two things need to be true. First, every write carries an idempotency key, so the same request produces the same result without duplicating the action:

```python
payload = {
    "event_id": "evt_482",
    "new_time": "2026-05-12T14:00",
    "idempotency_key": "run_847_step_3",
}
```

Second, after an ambiguous timeout, the agent should check the calendar before retrying. Verify first, retry second.

The **visible failure** is "the meeting got booked twice." The **deeper failure** is that the system treated all tool calls as equivalent, when reads and writes have fundamentally different retry semantics.

## Failure 6: Nobody can debug it

A user reports that the docs Q&A agent gave them a wrong answer about retention policy yesterday. You open the logs. You find the user's question, the agent's response, and one entry that says `tool_call: retrieval, status: success`.

That's not observability. That's timestamps with prose attached.

You can't tell which passages were retrieved, which were ranked highest, what the prompt looked like, what version of the prompt was deployed yesterday, or which of the agent's claims came from which passage. So you can't tell whether the failure was in retrieval ranking, in synthesis, in the prompt, or in a model regression.

A useful trace shows enough to localize the failure: input, relevant state, action chosen, tool inputs and outputs, verification result, state after, and version info for prompts and tools. Without that, the system isn't really running in production. *It's running in a black box that produces outputs.*

The **visible failure** is "we can't debug this." The **deeper failure** is that observability was treated as logging steps (what happened), not recording decisions (why). Article 5 in this series goes deeper into what useful traces actually contain.

## The pattern behind the failures

In each case, the system asked the model to carry control that should have lived somewhere else. The model was asked to remember operational facts from raw transcript. To decide whether a hedged claim was safe to act on. To pick a safe tool sequence. To know when evidence was strong enough. To retry safely after an ambiguous write. To be debuggable after the fact.

That's too much responsibility to leave inside the model.

A reliable agent system moves some of that responsibility out of the model and into the harness around it. The harness decides what the model sees, what actions are allowed, what gets verified, and what is preserved for next time.

**That's the core shift. Stop asking only what the model did wrong. Start asking where control should have lived.**

![Six failure shapes mapped to the control surfaces that should have caught them. The model is not the right place to fix any of these. The system around the model is.](failure_shapes.png){#fig-failure-shapes fig-alt="A mapping diagram with six agent failure shapes listed on one side and the control surfaces in the surrounding system that should have caught them on the other, with lines connecting each failure to its responsible surface. Every line lands on a system-level control rather than on the model itself, visually making the point that these failures are fixed in the harness around the model, not in the model."}

Most of the failures above don't get fixed by editing the prompt. State, workflow phase, tool design, retrieval, validators, approval gates, retry policy, traces, regression tests: these are the control surfaces around the language model.

The next four articles in this series go deeper on the most important ones:
- Article 2 makes the central reframe: an agent is the model plus the harness around it. That harness has four jobs: shape the input, bound the action, verify the outcome, and preserve the lesson.
- Article 3 is about state: the system memory that helps the harness shape what the model sees.
- Article 4 is about gates and tools: how the harness limits what actions are allowed, executes them safely, and verifies the outcome.
- Article 5 is about traces and the hardening loop: how the harness preserves what happened, learns from failure, and makes the system harder to break next time.

## What people get wrong

**1. Treating reliability as a model property.** When the agent fails, the instinct is to swap to a stronger model. Sometimes that helps. Often the failure comes back wearing a different costume because the harness around the model never changed.

**2. Confusing demos with evidence.** A demo proves the idea might work. It does not prove the system is ready. Demos run with one user, one clean conversation, one path through the workflow, and a developer watching every output. Production has none of those luxuries.

**3. Mistaking prompt rules for runtime enforcement.** A system prompt that says "do not do XYZ" is not a control surface. It is a hope. Real boundaries live in control gates, not the prompt.

**4. Logging steps instead of decisions.** Logs that record what happened are not the same as traces that record why. A line that says `tool_call: retrieval, status: success` does not help you debug a wrong answer.

**5. Believing failures are random.** Most aren't. The six failures in this article are recurring shapes that show up in different tasks. Once you've seen the pattern, you stop being surprised by individual failures and start recognizing them as instances of categories you can engineer against.

## References

- Anthropic. "Building Effective Agents." December 2024. <https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents>
- Anthropic. "Effective Context Engineering for AI Agents." 2025. <https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-context-engineering-for-ai-agents>
- Boeckeler, Birgitta. "The Engineering of AI Agents: Context, Harnessing, and Autonomy." InfoQ, May 2026. <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_R83pFpUWyM>
- Iusztin, Paul. "Agentic Harness Engineering." Decoding AI, 2025. <https://www.decodingai.com/p/agentic-harness-engineering>
